summary: The paper has improved significantly from earlier iterations. The Phi Modulator Framework is a genuine conceptual contribution; the central empirical finding (conditional indistinguishability under AdamW; 18.3x SGD/AdamW ratio) is correctly executed, statistically honest, and cross-validated from workspace data. The paper earns credit for rare candor: it explicitly scopes claims to ResNet-20 CIFAR scale, distinguishes non-significance from proved equivalence, and reports all results including null. However, four structural weaknesses remain critical. First, the paper's most important claim — the Phi Invariance Conjecture — is tested in a regime where it is mechanically almost guaranteed to hold (WD perturbation = O(0.001) of gradient update at lambda=5e-4 on ResNet-20); the conjecture's interesting regime boundary is asserted but never probed. Second, two of four claimed Phi modulation axes (spatial, target-norm) have zero experimental coverage, yet the paper claims to evaluate 'all major dynamic WD strategies spanning all four axes.' Third, statistical power at N=3 is insufficient for a null-result paper (MDE ~0.77%; TOST power ~15-20%). Fourth, the paper contains multiple internal inconsistencies requiring correction before external review: (1) a contradictory SGD swd significance status between the paper body (p=0.054, not significant) and the paper abstract/contributions section (where 'two comparisons achieve significance after Holm correction' citing swd with d=3.48 at p=0.004) arising from a Holm family-size disagreement (6 vs. 11 comparisons); (2) Cohen's d mislabeled as 'paired formula' while values match unpaired pooled; (3) Figure 4 uses reconstructed 'illustrative' trajectories as mechanistic evidence. The prior BEM=0.000 bug for half_lambda has been fixed in the analysis and paper reports corrected values. The SWD significance retraction is correctly handled. Current estimated paper quality: 6.0/10.

findings.description: The Phi Invariance Conjecture is tested exclusively in the regime where it holds almost trivially. At lambda=5e-4 on ResNet-20 (270K params) with AdamW, the WD perturbation |delta_WD| = lambda * norm(theta) ~ 5e-4 * 100 = 0.05, while the gradient step |delta_grad| ~ eta / sqrt(v_hat) ~ 1e-3 / 1.0 ~ 1e-3. The WD perturbation is ~5% of the gradient step per parameter per step — not vanishingly small. Yet the paper's own Section 4.2 states the Regime I bound requires the perturbation to be O(rho^2 * V * T * eta^2), i.e., very small when rho is small. The actual theoretical prediction is that invariance holds when rho ~ 0.5 and the paper observes exactly that. The conjecture's interesting claim is about Regimes II and III (rho >> 1), which are entirely untested. A reviewer will correctly note: at rho=0.5, AdamW's sign normalization nearly cancels all per-parameter differences in WD magnitude — the result is mechanically expected, not surprising. The conjecture becomes scientifically interesting only if it can be falsified at rho=5 or rho=50. Neither lambda-sweep (P1-1) nor BN ablation (Gate 1) has been run. Every major experimental gate is unexecuted.
findings.suggestion: Run at minimum the lambda-sweep experiment (P1-1): ResNet-20, CIFAR-10, AdamW, lambda in {5e-5, 5e-4, 5e-3, 5e-2}, 3 seeds each, constant vs. cosine_schedule vs. no_wd. This is ~12 GPU-hours and directly tests whether invariance breaks at rho=5 or rho=50. If it does, the paper gains a positive Regime II result. If it does not, the paper's claim of 'AdamW invariance at all tested rho values' becomes much stronger. Without this, the Trichotomy Conjecture's Regimes II and III are pure speculation with zero empirical support.

findings.description: The paper claims the Phi framework subsumes 'all major dynamic WD strategies along four modulation axes' and evaluates 'seven methods spanning four axes,' but two of four axes have zero experimental coverage: spatial (AlphaDecay, per-layer coefficients) and target-norm (AdamWN, per-parameter norm control). Table 1 catalogues these methods with a dagger symbol but they appear in no experiment. The null result ('all strategies equivalent under AdamW') explicitly cannot generalize to untested axes. Target-norm control (AdamWN) changes the WD force in a genuinely different way — it applies zero WD to already-small parameters and more WD to large ones — and could plausibly break invariance even at rho=0.5. If a reviewer knows that AdamWN has been shown to outperform constant WD in some settings, the four-axis coverage claim is falsified by the existing literature without running a single new experiment.
findings.suggestion: Either (1) add AdamWN to CIFAR benchmark (implementation: replace lambda*theta with lambda*max(0,1-tau/norm(theta_layer))*theta; ~6 runs, 3 seeds x 2 datasets) and then legitimately claim four-axis coverage; or (2) restrict all claims to 'temporal and directional modulation axes' and retitle Table 1 accordingly. Option 1 is strongly preferred — AdamWN is easy to implement and the result (whether it breaks invariance or not) is scientifically interesting.

findings.description: The SGD swd significance status is inconsistent between the experiments section (Table 3 and Section 6.2 of experiments.md) and the paper body (paper.md abstract and Section 6.2). The experiments section states 'only one pairwise comparison achieves significance: constant vs. no_wd (p_adj=0.002)' and lists swd p_adj=0.054 (not significant after Holm correction for 6 single-dataset comparisons). The paper.md abstract states 'two of six comparisons achieve significance after Holm correction (constant vs. no_wd: d=10.29, p_adj=0.002; constant vs. swd: d=3.48, p_adj=0.004)' — citing a different, cross-dataset Holm correction over 11 comparisons (raw p=0.000399 x 11 tests). Both analyses are internally consistent, but they answer different questions and are currently presented in conflicting sections of the same paper. The abstract and contribution list cite the 11-family result; the experiment section cites the 6-family result. A reviewer will catch this discrepancy immediately. Additionally, the paired t-test on the actual seed values (constant=[91.30, 91.18, 91.17] vs. swd=[90.57, 90.63, 90.93]) gives raw p=0.071 — not the p=0.000399 cited for the cross-dataset joint analysis. These reflect fundamentally different statistical framing decisions.
findings.suggestion: Choose ONE statistical framing and apply it consistently throughout the paper. The more conservative choice (Holm over 6 within-dataset comparisons) gives only one significant result (no_wd) and is more defensible for the single-architecture, single-dataset claim. The cross-dataset joint analysis (11 comparisons) can be reported as secondary. Whichever is chosen, all occurrences in abstract, contributions, experiments, and conclusion must agree.

findings.description: All four Gate experiments (Gate 0: theorem provability; Gate 1: BN ablation; Gate 2: VGG-16-BN full; Gate 3: CIFAR-100 SGD no_wd completion) are explicitly listed in the proposal as 'must complete before any paper writing,' yet the paper has been written with all gates pending. Gate 1 (BN ablation) directly gates all mechanistic causal claims in Sections 3 and 4 — the paper cannot distinguish AdamW's l_inf mechanism from BN's scale-invariance without it. Gate 2 (VGG-16-BN full, 72 runs) is needed for the cross-architecture generalization claim. Gate 3 (CIFAR-100 SGD no_wd) means the CIFAR-100 SGD analysis is incomplete (n=1 for no_wd). The paper acknowledges these limitations, but presenting a paper with all structural gates unexecuted at submission time is a reviewability problem.
findings.suggestion: Prioritize Gate 1 (BN ablation, ~1 GPU-hour, 18 runs) as the cheapest highest-value experiment. Gate 3 is 20 minutes. These two gates together close two critical holes in the mechanistic and statistical claims at minimal compute cost. Gate 2 (VGG) should follow. If writing is already underway, at minimum add the BN ablation result before any external submission.

findings.description: Statistical power is insufficient for a null-result paper. With N=3 seeds and within-method sigma ~0.25-0.32%, the MDE at 80% power is ~0.77%. The TOST equivalence claim at delta=0.5% has power ~15-20% (2 df). For effects in the range 0.3-0.7% — which would be practically meaningful — the study cannot detect them. The CIFAR-100 spread of 0.76% (cosine 63.42% vs. no_wd 62.66%) falls exactly at the detection boundary and cannot be interpreted. A null-result paper claiming 'all strategies are equivalent' carries a higher evidential burden than a positive-result paper, and N=3 does not meet that standard for a publication claiming practical guidance (Section 7.1's decision heuristic).
findings.suggestion: Minimum: run 2 additional seeds (789, 999) for constant, no_wd, and cwd_hard under AdamW on CIFAR-10 (6 runs, ~2 GPU-hours). Report formal TOST at delta=0.3% with N=5. The paper's own proposal (P0-2) specifies this; it has not been executed. This single action moves TOST power from 15% to ~55% for the central equivalence claim.

findings.description: The CSI metric is scientifically incoherent as currently used. The paper reports that CSI does not predict accuracy under AdamW (Spearman rho < 0.3), then argues this SUPPORTS the Phi Invariance Conjecture. But CSI was designed to measure dynamical coupling stability differences between methods. If CSI fails to differentiate methods under AdamW, there are two explanations: (1) the methods genuinely produce identical dynamics (supporting invariance), or (2) CSI is a bad metric. The paper assumes explanation (1) without testing (2). The cross-validation that would distinguish these — does CSI predict accuracy under SGD where methods DO differ? — is not performed. Furthermore, Appendix C (sensitivity analysis for CSI weights w1=0.4, w2=0.3, w3=0.3) is cited in the main text but does not exist in the manuscript. Any reader attempting to verify CSI robustness is blocked.
findings.suggestion: Compute Spearman rank correlation between CSI and accuracy under SGD (data exists in sgd_baseline/). If CSI predicts SGD rankings (rho > 0.5) but not AdamW rankings (rho < 0.3), this simultaneously validates CSI as a metric and provides cross-validator evidence that AdamW invariance is real. Add this analysis and either add Appendix C with the sensitivity analysis or remove the Appendix C citation.

findings.description: The 18.3x SGD/AdamW ratio (accuracy gap for weight decay presence-absence) is presented as a headline result, but the ratio conflates two independent effects: (1) the optimizer mechanism difference (AdamW l_inf constraint vs. SGD no constraint) and (2) the rho operating-point difference (rho_AdamW=0.5 vs. rho_SGD=0.05, a 10x difference). The Phi Invariance Conjecture predicts that at rho=0.5, even SGD would show small differences; but that prediction is never tested. The paper acknowledges this confound in Section 7.3 (Limitation 6), but this acknowledgment is buried after the result is cited prominently in the abstract, contributions, and conclusion. A reviewer could reasonably argue that the entire SGD vs. AdamW comparison is comparing apple orchards vs. orange groves (different rho values) and drawing conclusions about fruit types. Furthermore, the paper reports '18.3x' in the experiment section but '64x' in paper.md based on Cohen's d ratio (10.29/0.16=64x vs. 0.913/0.050=18.3x) — these are two different quantities (accuracy ratio vs. effect size ratio) that are both reported under the same label 'SGD/AdamW ratio' in different sections.
findings.suggestion: Standardize to one ratio definition. The effect-size ratio (64x, Cohen's d) and the accuracy-gap ratio (18.3x) measure different things and should be reported separately. Run the matched-rho SGD control experiment (P1-c in the proposal): SGD with lambda=5e-4 (same as AdamW), giving rho_SGD=0.05 at matched rho=0.5 to isolate the optimizer mechanism contribution. Until then, the headline SGD/AdamW comparison is a mixed-effects confound.

findings.description: Figure 4 uses 'illustrative' (reconstructed/interpolated) weight norm trajectories as primary mechanistic evidence. The figure footer states: 'Note: Trajectories are illustrative; final norms are documented experimental values.' Yet Section 6.4 uses these trajectories as evidence for 'the l_inf constraint mechanism: AdamW's implicit constraint absorbs weight decay perturbations, driving all trajectories to a common neighborhood.' This is a critical scientific integrity issue: the visual smoothness of the convergence trajectories (the core visual argument for the mechanism) is a property of the interpolation, not of the measured data. The actual epoch_metrics.jsonl training logs contain measured weight norms per epoch and must be used instead.
findings.suggestion: Regenerate Figure 4 from actual epoch_metrics.jsonl data. The script already exists in the workspace. If actual trajectories show noise/non-monotonic behavior, that is scientifically honest and acceptable — report them as measured. Alternatively, replace Figure 4 with a bar chart of final weight norms only (documented real values: 95.89-97.04 for AdamW, much wider for SGD), removing all trajectory curves.

findings.description: Cohen's d formula is mislabeled throughout. Section 5.3 claims 'Cohen's d is computed as the paired formula d = delta_bar / s_delta where delta_bar is the mean difference and s_delta is the standard deviation of differences.' Verification against actual seed data shows the reported values match the UNPAIRED POOLED formula: d = (mu1 - mu2) / sqrt((s1^2 + s2^2) / 2). For SGD SWD vs. constant (seeds [91.30,91.18,91.17] vs. [90.57,90.63,90.93]): paired d = 2.04, unpaired pooled d = 3.478 — the paper reports 3.48, matching the unpaired pooled formula. For no_wd: paired d = 12.17, unpaired pooled d = 10.287 — paper reports 10.29. The hypothesis table in hypotheses.md confirms both values: 'Cohen's d (paired) = -12.17; pooled d = -10.29.' The paper reports the pooled value but labels it as 'paired.' This affects the comparison with other papers that may use either convention.
findings.suggestion: Correct Section 5.3: describe the unpaired pooled formula d = (mu1-mu2)/sqrt((s1^2+s2^2)/2). Note that p-values use paired t-test (to exploit matched-seed design) while d uses the standard unpaired pooled convention for cross-study comparability. Also correct Table 3 cwd_hard d from 1.08 to 1.13 (the sgd_baseline_analysis.json reports 1.1327).

findings.description: The BEM=0.000 bug for half_lambda has been acknowledged and fixed in the paper (reported as -0.500 in Table 2), but the pipeline implementation still computes 0.000 because half_lambda is implemented as lambda=2.5e-4 with phi=1 rather than lambda=5e-4 with phi=0.5. Any replicator running the raw pipeline will get BEM=0.000 for half_lambda, disagreeing with the paper's Table 2 value of -0.500. This is a reproducibility gap that cannot be resolved by a footnote alone — the footnote says 'corrected' but the code is not corrected.
findings.suggestion: Fix the half_lambda implementation to use phi=0.5 (constant modulator at 0.5 for all parameters) with base lambda=5e-4. Rerun 6 experiments (3 seeds x 2 datasets). This eliminates the footnote entirely and ensures pipeline reproducibility. The proposal's P0-2 specifies this; it was not executed.

findings.description: Figure 3 CIFAR-100 panel shows Pearson r=0.48 with a visible upward trend line, but the panel subtitle reads 'No correlation in Regime I.' This is factually incorrect: r=0.48 is a moderate positive correlation, not 'no correlation.' The 'no correlation' characterization applies only to the CIFAR-10 panel (r=-0.05). This is a straightforward factual error visible to any reviewer.
findings.suggestion: Update the CIFAR-100 panel title to 'CIFAR-100: moderate trend (r=0.48, non-significant at n=3)' or similar. Reserve 'No correlation' for CIFAR-10 panel only.

findings.description: Section 1.3 parenthetical blends AdamW and SGD settings in a confusing way: 'at AdamW settings (AdamW: lambda=5e-4, eta=1e-3, rho=0.5; SGD: lambda=5e-3, eta=0.1, rho=0.05), seven weight decay strategies...produce statistically indistinguishable results under AdamW.' The SGD rho=0.05 appears to describe the 'AdamW settings' context, which will cause readers to wonder why SGD settings are being provided for an AdamW result. This is a clarity issue that reviewers unfamiliar with the paper will stumble on.
findings.suggestion: Restructure: 'At rho=0.5 (AdamW: lambda=5e-4, eta=1e-3), all seven strategies are statistically indistinguishable (Figure 1). Under SGD (rho=0.05, lambda=5e-3, eta=0.1), weight decay presence produces a massive effect (d>10, Figure 2).'

findings.description: The Phi framework's taxonomic contribution is genuine but risks 'notation, not theory' reception. Proposition 1 (composition) is trivially true. The four-axis taxonomy, while useful for organizing experiments, is primarily a systematization of the existing literature rather than a new theoretical result. D'Angelo et al. (2024) already established the primary empirical conclusion (WD irrelevance under AdamW+BN) for the static case; our dynamic case is an extension but not a qualitative departure. The strongest scientific contribution is actually the SGD/AdamW effect-size ratio and the explicit rho regime framing — these are underpublicized positive findings that should be foregrounded more in the abstract.
findings.suggestion: In the abstract and introduction, lead with the empirical finding (18.3x ratio, SGD significance structure) as the primary contribution, framing the Phi framework as the enabling infrastructure. Currently the abstract presents the framework as primary and the empirical result as secondary — this is the wrong emphasis given the novelty distribution.

metric_gaming_risks: The AdamW null result is not at risk of metric gaming — the direction of concern is opposite: insufficient power to detect a real effect that exists. The 0.76% CIFAR-100 spread is at the detection edge and may mask a real BN-architecture-specific effect.

The cosine_schedule variance reduction (sigma=0.07% vs. ~0.30% for other methods, CIFAR-10) is reported from N=3. Variance estimates from 3 samples have extremely wide confidence intervals and should not be reported as a 'finding' without Levene's test or a larger sample.

The SGD swd result presents a significance inconsistency depending on Holm family size (6 within-dataset vs. 11 cross-dataset comparisons). The paper currently cherry-picks the more significant result (11-family, p=0.004) in the abstract while reporting the conservative result (6-family, p=0.054) in the experiment section. Both are defensible analyses, but mixing them is misleading.
novelty_risks: D'Angelo et al. (2024) NeurIPS showed WD irrelevance under AdamW+BN (static case). A reviewer familiar with this work will argue the dynamic-WD null result is a direct corollary. The paper's differentiation — dynamic strategies, rho regime framework, cross-optimizer comparison — must be stated more crisply.

Kosson et al. (2023) rotational equilibrium under AdamW partially predicts the invariance finding. The paper should more explicitly discuss what the invariance finding adds beyond Kosson et al.'s result.

The Phi four-axis taxonomy may have been anticipated by CWD (Chen et al., 2026a) related work section. If CWD's related work already organizes the literature along similar axes, the framework's novelty is more narrow than claimed.
reproducibility_gaps: Appendix C (CSI sensitivity analysis with varied w1/w2/w3 weights) is cited in Section 3.4 but does not exist in the current manuscript. Any reader attempting to verify CSI robustness is blocked.

SGD CIFAR-100 full results table is absent. The underlying data exists in exp/results/sgd_baseline/cifar100/ but is documented only as inline claims ('SWD p=0.036'). A replicator cannot verify the full CIFAR-100 SGD analysis from the paper alone.
BEM for half_lambda: pipeline computes 0.000 but paper reports -0.500. A replicator running the code will get the wrong number.

Figure 4 trajectories are labeled 'illustrative' (reconstructed), not measured. The smooth convergence curves that anchor the mechanistic argument cannot be reproduced from the workspace data by following the figure generation scripts.

CIFAR-100 SGD no_wd has n=1 (seed_42 only). The no_wd entry in any cross-dataset statistical comparison is single-seed. Gate 3 (run seed_123, ~20 min) was identified as required but has not been executed.

Inconsistency in total experiment count: paper.md abstract claims '87+ experiments'; experiment section specifies 42 AdamW + 42 SGD + 3 VGG pilot = 87 runs. Both agree on 87, but 7x2x3=42 SGD assumes all 7 methods on CIFAR-100 completed for SGD — the analysis JSON shows CIFAR-100 no_wd n=1 for SGD, suggesting at most 41 complete CIFAR-100 SGD runs (missing 2 seeds for no_wd). The '87+' is approximately correct but the '42 SGD' claim overstates by 2 runs.
